data.title: AXIA: Key Infrastructure For AI, Beyond Electrical Potential
data.description:
AXIA Energia (AXIA) is a Brazilian electric company with the largest installed capacity in South America, with 44.4 GW. AXIA focuses on generation and transmission, but not distribution; therefore, it is not necessary to measure the number of users it supplies, but rather the strength of its energy infrastructure. Much of that generation and transmission power that is AXIA was explained in my previous article, where I also detailed why it was my bet on Brazil for 2026.

Brazil (EWZ), which rose almost 30% so far in 2026, is a market that has always caught my attention due to its big economic potential, as it is the largest country on the continent (after the U.S.). There are a lot of interesting sectors to invest in, but because I like the investments in energy infrastructure, I set my eyes on AXIA. However, today I want to show you the other key part of my thesis on the company, which is also connected to electric power: the opportunities I see for AXIA to become the nexus with hyperscalers to be the source of AI infrastructure in South America.

AXIA has risen 41.23% since the publication of my article on December 24, 2025, compared to 2.86% for the S&P 500 (SP500). The return occurred in a context of high volatility due to the conflict in the Middle East. Besides, the company presented very good results during Q4 2025; it maintained its profitability outlook thanks to the new fee pricing scheme, and it raised its investment expectations.

I added to this the future potential to supply AI development, and that's why I continue to give a Buy recommendation for AXIA. I understand those who invested at the end of 2025 or earlier in the company are analyzing the possibility of lowering their exposure and taking some profits. In my case, I believe there is still more potential ahead.

If a hyperscaler lands in Brazil, it will need to shake AXIA's hand

It's true there's nothing settled on commercial agreements. But in the conference call after the Q4 2025 earnings presentation, management said they are having talks with hyperscalers to supply AI data centers. The challenges are structural. In other words: they need more energy and better operational efficiency for the AI infrastructure to work more efficiently. Italo Freitas, Vice-President of AXIA, said:

We are talking to some data center players, large, major players, and the question is that today, all of the infrastructure challenge that Brazil needs to adapt in order to be able to receive this big data center, a large data center, hyperscale, more than 500 mega, or 1 gigawatt, or 1.5 gigawatts, it brings operational challenges to the system, especially in the application, if it's a training application or inference. If it's training, it may simply stop overnight and the frequency of the system may go down, and ONS is not prepared for that.

So there is a structural challenge that must be adjusted to receive these big loads. But naturally, Brazil, especially the Northeast, it's a region that will help a lot to have a large load. So we are talking to some players and looking at all of these points, the operational points that need to be adjusted.

Conference call, February 27, 2026

While I see the energy crisis in the Strait of Hormuz, I think that investing in electricity through diversified sources is the best investment. But as I said: AXIA is not just a generator, it also carries large energy loads on its transmission lines. Transmission is key to sustaining the high voltages that AI data centers generate. Therefore, if in the future a mega data center is built in Brazil, it's hard to think there won't be an agreement before with AXIA.

And this is very important because you can see it in the valuation multiples. Large utilities already have contracts with hyperscalers, as do some former cryptocurrency miners that are transitioning into AI hosting. In those companies, the contracts are translated into premium prices. On the other hand, part of my search for energy investment is based on anticipating future deals with hyperscalers. I believe AXIA, having nothing closed yet, still trades at accessible prices. Meanwhile, the recent rise is based on the other part of my investment thesis: the company's mega operational capacity.

AXIA in the transmission investment boom

Anyway, I don't think AXIA will fail if it isn't the AI's power source. I mean: if there are no agreements with hyperscalers in 2026, it doesn't change my buying thesis. And I arrived at that conclusion because electricity demand will grow due to several sectors in the coming years, as I explained in some of my recent articles, among them the one about American Electric Power (AEP). In that article I said:

"Electric companies have a long term catalyst: AI data centers that need too much energy to

work. I believe many companies in the sector have already priced in that potential. CAPEX’s projection for the workload in AI data centers is $1.3 trillion. But electricity demand growth projections will not be limited to data centers only.

The population is consuming more and more electricity due to economic growth, the replacement of gas heating, industrial use, EVs, among other factors. In the growth outlook, industrial and commercial demand will keep representing more than 50% of consumption, followed by residential consumption".

March 24, 2026

Therefore, in this global electrification boom, large generators with efficient infrastructure could become increasingly profitable. The electrification boom is not a new interpretation. But what I try to do in my investments is to look for opportunities with the greatest potential and without premium valuations that discount future profitability. So here comes AXIA, because it is a foreign alternative and operates in a volatile and politically unstable market like Brazil, it means a lower discount premium.

Therefore, what I see is that value outweighs risks. For example, in that high demand for future electrification, transmission networks need to be renewed. Most investments in the coming years will be in transmission until they equal investments in distribution by 2028. In summary, investments in transmission were 44% in 2020 and will increase to 50% in 2028. I believe that whoever takes advantage of this segment will gain more profitability.

AXIA has 74,000 km of transmission lines and represents the largest part of the investment since 2022. That investment went from R$ 1.49 billion in 2022 to R$ 4.76 billion in 2025, while another rise is expected for 2026. In the following image you will see the weight of transmission.

You can also see the breakdown of recent tenders on the right. The most recent is the ANEEL auction for the construction and maintenance of transmission lines, which adds R$1.6 billion and a RAP of R$140 million. In total, the CAPEX of the last four years is providing AXIA with an RAP of R$1.8 billion, with the predictability of regulated revenue, which ensures a return on investment. Total investments in Q4 2025 were R$3.9 billion, a 28% YoY increase. In 2025, they totaled R$9.6 billion and are expected to reach between R$12 and R$14 billion annually in 2026 and 2027.

2025 earnings: cost efficiency and focus on transmission

The best of Q4 2025, besides the long term view, I believe was the efficiency in PMSO. Those costs have kept declining since the privatization of AXIA in 2022, and I believe that is another key part of its finances. This is because as investment increases, margins could decrease in the short term. Therefore, cost control becomes crucial.

PMSO was R$ 1.69 billion in Q4 2025 compared to R$ 2.35 billion in the same period of the previous year (slide 41 of the Earnings Release). Based on what the management's said, AI is helping to lower PMSOs, but there is still plenty of room for improvement.

Revenue grew 3.1% in 2025, to R$ 41.42 billion, with strong growth in gross transmission revenue of 4.3%. Meanwhile, gross generation revenues fell by 0.8%. As I explained before, the transmission segment is the engine of investment, as well as revenue.

As a result, the company is in a transition from a more volatile business (generation) to a more stable and regulated business (transmission). The company was selling off thermal power plants last year, trying to lower costs, which in the short term meant less generation. I said generation is more volatile because it depends more on consumption. On the other hand, transmission is adjusted to regulated prices.

Both segments will remain in AXIA's portfolio, but I'm seeing this shift towards transmission as an opportunity. And this is key in my thesis, as I explained before, because if any agreement with hyperscalers isn't reached, AXIA has a predictable, stable income. But the best of all is that it improves the margins. The following image (slide 9) clearly shows the improvement in efficiency, where its regulatory EBITDA and its adjusted regulatory EBITDA grew 17.1% and 12.9% YoY, respectively.

At the same time, as you can see on the left side of the image, gross revenue dropped sharply, clearly showing that transition. The sale of assets represented R$ 997 million less in revenue due to lower generation, which was partially offset by the 9.7% increase in energy prices. But the important thing for me is margins. As you can see on slide 16, the total generation margin increased from R$ 1.47 billion in Q4 2024 to R$ 2.15 billion. This is due to improved efficiency, but also to the ACL (free contracting environment) market.

There is still potential in AXIA's shares

Despite the rally in 2026 (+40%), AXIA shares still trade at a discount compared to other major players in the utilities sector. For example, NextEra (NEE), Constellation Energy (CEG), and Southern Company (SO) are trading at EV/EBITDA FWD multiples of 15.58, 12.68, and 12.82, respectively. Meanwhile, AXIA's EV/EBITDA FWD is trading between approximately 8x and 9x. Even more: when I wrote my previous article last December, EV/EBITDA FWD was in the same range. This is explained by the projected EBITDA growth of 15.5% for AXIA.

I don't feel very comfortable giving target prices, which is why in my previous article I didn't give any target price, although I did mention discounts compared to the sector, such as the 32.2% discount on the value per book. Now, that discount is 13% (1.9x versus 2.2x). As I explained at the beginning, the rise of more than 40% so far this year could be a good moment to take some profits. I would really understand those who take that path.

But for my part, I not only feel comfortable maintaining my position, but I'm buying even more. If EV/EBITDA rises to a modest 12x or 13x, the price could reach $19 or $21, in line with the nearly 50% increase in the multiple.

Risks

In my last article I explained the risks of investing in AXIA, which were related to the political and economic context of Brazil. I mentioned the interest rates well above inflation and the presidential elections in October. I won't retell them. I'll focus on expanding on those I already mentioned. Specifically two.

First, Brazil's fiscal deficit, that is usually high. In 2025 it was around 8.3% of the GDP. During the presidency of Jair Bolsonaro, it dropped to approximately 4%. Despite the budget shortfall, I believe Lula is putting the public finances on the agenda for a possible fourth term, according to Reuters. He would do it through budget cuts and limits on tax breaks.

The other risk I consider for AXIA is weather-related, something I didn't write in my previous article. Droughts affect hydrology, and therefore, thermal power plants, which are still key to the generation segment. On slide 16, I found that the company anticipates a second half of the year where poor hydrology could impact generation revenues, especially in the third quarter. I believe we should follow this item closely, even though it doesn't change the core principles of my long-term thesis.

Conclusion

I'm taking advantage of this bargain in the utilities sector, which has already risen more than 40% so far in 2026. Is it still a bargain? I believe so for several reasons: massive operating capacity, significant investment in transmission lines, and projected stable revenues in both the regulated transmission market and the free market for generation. But is not just that. AXIA could become the key nexus for Brazil to host the AI ​​infrastructure that hyperscalers need in their data centers. I think that's a bonus that the market hasn't yet priced in because there's no public agreement in place. Finally, the EV/EBITDA FWD multiples are not only below those of the major utilities, but also below the sector average. AXIA remains my top investment in Brazil for 2026, and perhaps in all of South America within the electric utilities sector.

data.title: Managing The Cognitive Dissonance Of Long-Term Investing
data.description:
By Brian Levitt, Chief Global Market Strategist and Head of Strategy & Insights

Life entails a certain level of cognitive dissonance. It’s the discomfort you feel when you hold two conflicting beliefs at the same time - and the way your brain resolves that discomfort by rationalizing behavior.

I wrote this one day before my 50th birthday, a milestone that has me rationalizing behavior that conflicts with what I know to be true.

Belief A: Alcohol is bad for my health.

Belief B: I’m going to drink alcohol on my birthday.

The rationalization is simple. It’s for one night. What matters most to my long-term health are decisions made over decades, not over single nights.

Investing presents a similar tension, but with far higher stakes.

Belief A: Trying to time the market is bad for my investing health.

Belief B: Reducing exposure until risks subside will make me feel safer.

The rationalization many investors often reach is confidence in their own intuition. “I’ll know when to get back in. I’ll recognize the signal. I’ll avoid the worst of the decline and still participate fully in the recovery.”

The difference between these two examples is stark. In the first case, I may wake up tired and dehydrated. In the second, investors may permanently erode my long-term investment returns. The cost of resolving that discomfort is much higher.

Market movements in an uncertain world

Markets rarely reward those who wait for certainty. By the time risks feel resolved, prices have often already adjusted. For the most part, markets are forward-looking and probabilistic versus reactive and emotional.

Recent events provide a clear example. Despite elevated geopolitical risk, markets have behaved as though a worst-case outcome was unlikely. The S&P 500 Index bottomed on March 30 following a peak-to-trough decline of “only” 9.01%.1 They appeared to be looking through the conflict in the Middle East, anticipating a relatively short duration and limited economic spillovers.

So, when Iran announced on Friday that the Strait of Hormuz would remain open during the ceasefire, it felt like confirmation rather than surprise.2 Oil prices, which had been primarily falling since the end of March, plunged.3 The S&P 500 Index, which had been rallying for most of the month, closed at an all-time high.4 Small-cap and emerging market stocks extended gains that began in mid-March.5 Credit spreads tightened further, and are now lower than the days before the conflict began.6

Forward-looking versus reactive

Markets being forward-looking and probabilistic for the most part, rather than reactive and emotional, is an important distinction. Waiting on the sidelines for Friday’s announcement about the opening of the Strait of Hormuz may not have been rewarded. A lot of positive price action came before the good news and continued Friday, before cautious investors were likely able to respond to it. Weekend events resulted in the Strait being closed again, but the point still holds. While there may be near-term volatility as a result, the market may still trade as if a negotiated deal is forthcoming.

Importantly, many fundamentals appeared to reinforce this market resilience. Corporate earnings were off to a solid start, particularly among large banks.7 Jobless claims remained low.8 Regional purchasing manager surveys have been moving higher, suggesting firmer manufacturing momentum.9 Growth in the United Kingdom has exceeded expectations.10 Growth in China has also come in better than the most pessimistic forecasts.11

Many investors tend to stay invested because they accept cognitive dissonance as part of the process. They recognize that their time horizon is longer than the lifespan of any single conflict. They think in probabilities rather than certainties. Much like decisions in life, investment decisions are best judged over decades, not nights.

data.title: RYLD: When Beta Fails, Income Delivers
data.description:
Since I issued a Buy on the Global X Russell 2000 Covered Call ETF (RYLD) in July last year, the call has gone both right and wrong. In total return terms, RYLD has returned ~11% but has lagged the underlying Russell 2000 significantly (rallied ~21%). I had envisioned a flat market for the Russell 2000, where RYLD's aggressive option positioning reaps the maximum reward over the underlying; that has not happened with small caps rallying strongly (through volatility).

RYLD's option coverage is quite aggressive, and small-cap index options usually trade with greater premiums (because of greater underlying volatility). This makes RYLD a powerful option income strategy by virtue of its positioning. So, even if it captures partial upside when small caps rally, aggressive income in markets that are flat to slightly down makes up for the opportunity losses. Investors should only brace for a scenario where small caps undergo a deep correction: this is a zone where conviction in long-term rebounds is required. RYLD's yield is just about double digits (~12% on a TTM basis), somewhat sustainable through a combination of capital appreciation (although capped) and option income in the long term. So NAV erosion is not a systemic problem here, and rebounds can be capitalized on as long as the direction stays upward bound.

Over the next few quarters, I expect the small-cap space to remain range-bound (or slightly under pressure) as the odds of lower growth and a stickier inflation scenario are high. In both scenarios, RYLD's aggressive option income and drawdown mitigation (limited but strong) should be able to actually show alpha over the Russell 2000 (something I expected last July). Hence, RYLD continues to be a Buy. The large downside scenario cannot be ruled out, but despite the small-cap outperformance over the last 9 months, valuations are at historical lows compared to the large-cap space. That leaves room for mean reversion once the macro visibility improves.

Portfolio Structure

RYLD has a straightforward methodology. It aggressively writes options on the full underlying Russell 2000 portfolio (long Global X Russell 2000 ETF). I checked this by downloading the full holdings from the ETF website and found 5,219 short calls on the Russell 2000 index. The notional value of this exposure is ~$1.3 billion (5,219 contracts x 100 multiplier x 2,465 strike), matching the fund's AUM. The choice of strikes is important, but this does not look like an active call because RYLD tracks the CBOE Russell 2000 BuyWrite Index, and BuyWrite indices generally write options ATM. Barring slight interpretation of ATM strike (rounding) discrepancies, I do not see a lot of active calls going into the strategy.

That means high premiums due to both the ATM positioning as well as elevated premiums anyway. For context, you can look at the ATM option premiums for the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) and SPY. I checked them as of April 17th, 2026, and for SPY, the ATM strike of $702 is trading at ~$14 for the May 22nd, 2026 expiry. For IWM, the $270 strike for the same expiry is trading at ~$7.7. That is ~2% income for SPY ATM options and ~2.9% for IWM ATM options. The exact differences will differ depending on how volatile markets become (gaps are likely to only increase).

The Downside Argument

Despite the small-cap outperformance over the past 9 months, they are still trading at a deep relative discount to larger caps. Most of the relative apathy has been a result of relative strength in large caps rather than historical weakness in small caps. That has started to change. Since September-October last year, the large-cap space, dominated by tech and AI themes by market cap, has started cooling. That has seen a lot of rotation into international and value markets. Some of the rotation has spilled over to the broader markets as well. As the tech-AI play continues to await a fresh round of catalysts (i.e., confirmation of cyclical strength in semis and equipment, CapEx digestion and monetization in hyperscalers, and pricing power confirmation in SaaS), the relative rotation into small caps should also keep sharp downsides at bay over the next 2-3 quarters at least.

We have discussed this in my earlier thesis too, but here is fresh evidence of what happens if sharp drawdowns do open up. During the peak of the Middle East crisis, when IWM was down by over 8%, RYLD was down by 2% less and theoretically could have earned back that ~6% drawdown too if markets had decided to stay at the lows (through 1-2% option income over the next few months). The drawdown mitigation is limited, but RYLD's ATM strike positioning, 100% portfolio coverage, and higher option premiums, especially in volatile environments, make it a relatively better covered call writing vehicle when it comes to the magnitude of drawdown mitigation. However, sharp downsides still remain a zone for worry for investors — a zone where the strategy becomes less market-agnostic and relies on a long-term rebound eventually. I do not see odds of a very sharp downside ahead: any drawdown of up to 10% can be relatively absorbed over a few months, the total return cushioned by the income if not distributed.

If I Get It Wrong

That is what happened since July last year. And this is a happy scenario to be in. Investors claim a lot less of the rally, but they see NAVs steady and payouts still funded by underlying capital appreciation — opportunity loss but income and NAV both intact.

The Base Scenario

The base scenario is, of course, where I see the markets headed. Low growth and sticky inflation could mean small-cap stocks face higher rate sensitivity (due to more floating rate and short-duration debt that needs refinancing). In a lower growth environment, the relative lack of brand strength often means weaker pricing power and an inability to pass on higher input costs, pressuring margins. If the funding environment pulls back due to higher credit risks, that too increases funding pressures on small caps. The combined weights in energy and materials (relative winners in a stagflation scenario) are just about double digits, so the support is limited if a stagflation-like scenario emerges.

On the contrary, a flat to slightly pressured environment for small caps often comes with higher volatility, which means that a tougher time for the underlying translates to a reasonably strong income generation regime for RYLD. And this is what happens when the small-cap space ends flat (of course through rallies and corrections, i.e., higher volatility). In theory, RYLD should easily add 1-2% a month if the Russell 2000 does not rally from here. In practice, higher realized volatility than implied could mean less option premium capture over time. Still, RYLD could add 5-10% (depending on volatility and regime specifics) over a year - the flat conditions from October to March (through extreme volatility, of course) saw ~2.5% gains in RYLD in total return terms.

Overall, RYLD retains a Buy because the option layer is aggressively poised to capture gains from a flat market end-to-end and through possible volatility. The only scenario investors need to watch for is a sharp drawdown, which, though unlikely, could mean a more long-term wait for the strategy to pull back.

data.title: Nektar Therapeutics: 'Strong Buy' On REZPEG POC Established In AD/AA And Late-Stage Development
data.description:
The last time I spoke about Nektar Therapeutics (NKTR) it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Nektar Therapeutics: 2 Data Readouts For REZPEG 2025 Could Establish Further POC." With respect to this article, I mentioned that this company was in the process of developing its IL-2 receptor agonist/regulatory T-cell modulator rezpegaldesleukin [REZPEG or NKTR-358] for the treatment of patients with atopic dermatitis [AD] in the ongoing phase 2b REZOLVE-AD study. Plus, the ongoing development of this drug candidate in the ongoing phase 2b REZOLVE-AA study for the treatment of patients with alopecia areata [AA]. I'm happy to report that the company was indeed able to establish proof of concept [POC] that its IL-2 receptor agonist was indeed able to help treat both of these patients with these T-cell-driven immune disorders. As a matter of fact, the stock has gained 915.71% since my prior article. First and foremost, based on the positive outcome of both of these phase 2b studies, I believe that I should upgrade this stock from a "Buy" rating the last time around to a "Strong Buy."

Why do I believe that further gains are possible, despite the huge rise in share price? The biggest reason of all is that there are several catalysts this year and early into the next based on both of these programs. In terms of the phase 2b REZOLVE-AD study using REZPEG for the treatment of patients with AD, it is expected to move over to the phase 3 ZENITH-AD program and will initiate in Q2 of 2026. Plus, 36-week maintenance data from the phase 2b REZOLVE-AD study in AD patients is going to be presented at a medical conference in the 2nd half of 2026. Along with 52-week off-treatment data from this very same study in Q1 of 2027. Nektar previously reported positive results from its phase 2b REZOLVE-AA study treating induction patients over a 16-week period, having demonstrated mean Eczema Area and Severity Index [EASI] scores as compared to placebo. The latest update, which I'm going to go over in detail below, is that the company ran a blinded 16-week treatment extension period for this trial, and it reported positive data from it.

The T regulatory cell mechanism of REZPEG was shown to improve patients in terms of achieving a SALT Score ≤20 over time. This proves that continued treatment with this drug was able to help these severe-to-very-severe AA patients. Further milestones are expected for this program as well, which is why I am bullish. First and foremost, the results from REZOLVE-AA are to be presented at an upcoming medical conference in 2026. Along with the potential to see if patients continue to benefit from REZPEG while off of treatment. Topline data from 24-weeks of off-treatment from this specific trial is expected in Q4 of 2026. I haven't even dived into the fact that this IL-2 receptor agonist might also work well in being able to slow the progression of new-onset Type 1 Diabetes [T1D] by increasing regulatory T cells [Tregs]. With positive data from the phase 2b REZOLVE-AA study using REZPEG to treat patients with AA, plus the advancement of this very same drug to treat patients with AD, I believe that I should upgrade this stock from a "Buy" to a "Strong Buy." This is based on both progress to date and several milestones still expected to come about.

Latest REZPEG Data Reinforces Ability To Use T Regulatory Cells For T-Cell Driven Immune Disorders

As I mentioned above, this company has been able to make significant progress since I last wrote about it. It was able to achieve positive results from its phase 2b REZOLVE-AD study using REZPEG for the treatment of patients with atopic dermatitis [AD]. With that said, it is expected to initiate the phase 3 ZENITH program in Q2 of 2026 for this indication. What I want to go over today is the newly released blinded 16-week treatment extension of the phase 2b REZOLVE-AA study, using this IL-2 receptor agonist to treat patients with alopecia areata [AA]. Specifically, as I will point out below, continuation of twice-monthly subcutaneous [SC] injections for these patients resulted in more of them being able to achieve a SALT score ≤20. Before going over the clinical data program in detail, plus any catalysts to come about for this very specific AA treatment program, I believe it is important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

Alopecia areata [AA] is characterized as a chronic autoimmune disorder where the immune system specifically attacks the hair follicles of a patient. This is very bad for the patients, because they have hair loss that occurs in patches [non-scarring]. This is as a result of the immune system, which includes T-cells, natural killer [NK] cells, and auto-reactive CD8+ T-cells. Corticosteroids might help reduce inflammation, and there is also a case use for these patients to be given JAK inhibitors. However, while JAK inhibitors provide efficacy, they have problems in terms of safety with multiple Blackbox warnings [Nektar Therapeutics Corporate Presentation January 2026, slide #40]. The main item to note is that current biologics have high relapse rates along with safety problems. Thus, they are not durable for these AA patients. REZPEG is believed to offer not only a safer alternative but one that works very well. There is an opportunity here for this to become the first safe and effective biologic to treat patients with AA. Besides these patients suffering from hair patch loss, it gets even worse for them. How so? Well, that's because the hair loss causes them to lose their quality of life [QoL]. That is, they experience depression and/or anxiety because of the substantial patch of hair loss that comes with AA. The global alopecia areata market size is projected to reach $6.92 billion by 2034. This is a very large market opportunity for sure, and the company is indeed looking to offer REZPEG as a 1st-line treatment. However, the main proportion of patients the company is going after are those who have severe-to-very-severe disease.

In order to see whether or not REZPEG would be effective at being able to treat these patients with severe-to-very-severe AA, the company is in the process of running the phase 2b REZOLVE-AA study. This particular mid-stage trial recruited up to a total of 92 patients and then randomized them 3:3:2 to receive either of the following doses twice monthly as SC doses:

  • 35 patients given 24 µg/kg REZPEG
  • 37 patients given 18 µg/kg REZPEG
  • 20 patients given placebo

The primary endpoint was that of mean percentage SALT reduction from baseline. With respect to this efficacy endpoint, the drug was able to perform equally as well in two different groups [Nektar Phase 2b REZOLVE-AA Presentation at AAD 2026, slide #6]. One group of patients set forth as part of the main intent-to-treat [mITT] population and MITT patients with eligibility violations. That's the basis for the primary endpoint that was set forth. However, what's intriguing is this first data set from the 36-week period that was concluded, compared to that of the extended data.

What I mean by this is that in the 36 week study data there was something else that was revealed, which was the percentage of patients who achieved a SALT Score ≤20. What does this specific score mean? It means the percentage of patients who achieved a ≤20% scalp patch hair loss [80% or more scalp hair coverage in place, which is good for the patient]. With that said, at 36 weeks, the breakdown for the doses noted above was as follows [Nektar Phase 2b REZOLVE-AA Presentation at AAD 2026, slide #8]:

  • 15.6% of patients who took high dose 24 µg/kg REZPEG achieved SALT Score ≤20
  • 14.8% of patients who took low dose 18 µg/kg REZPEG achieved SALT Score ≤20
  • 6.7% of patients who took placebo achieved SALT Score ≤20

This is a very good finding, but the latest updated data released from the company specifically notes that patients treated in the 16-week blinded treatment extension period out to Week 52 saw continued improvement. However, it is important to state that the only patients allowed to roll over into this blinded extension period were those that had achieved a SALT score over 20 in the induction phase. The final outcome was this specific group of patients who continued to receive twice-monthly SC REZPEG had achieved an increase in SALT Score ≤20. With the 24 µg/kg REZPEG treatment, the percentage of patients who achieved a SALT Score ≤20 increased from 15.6% to 27.6% at Week 52. The same thing happened with the lower dose of 18 µg/kg REZPEG, where the percentage of patients achieving this measure increased from 14.8% to 25.8%. The point is that twice-monthly dosing of this drug continued to have an effect on improving patients with respect to this efficacy measure. The beauty of REZPEG is the mechanism of action [MOA] of it in treating AA along with other T-cell-driven autoimmune disorders. The point of it is to bind to the receptors of regulatory T cells [Tregs] and proliferate them. With the increase in Tregs, this brings about immune balance for starters. But even more importantly, it helps counter the immune privilege collapse that occurs in this disease. In AA, there is an immune privilege collapse of the anagen hair bulb, and this results in hair follicle inflammation thanks to the following:

  • NKG2D + T cells
  • NK cells
  • Auto-reactive CD8+ T cells

The point is that REZPEG drives Tregs in place in order to counter cytokine-driven inflammation. Especially since Tregs act upstream of these specific cytokines. The thought process is that increasing Tregs reduces such inflammatory activity of the anagen hair bulb. This MOA has been proven in the phase 2b REZOLVE-AA study.

The company has several catalysts expected to occur in 2026 and in 2027 as well. However, just looking at this program alone in targeting patients with AA, there are milestones for investors to keep an eye on. The first of which is that data from the phase 2b REZOLVE-AA study using REZPEG to treat these severe-to-very-severe AA are expected to be presented at an upcoming medical presentation in 2026. The notion of this drug is that it might offer a new safe/effective and durable biologic option for these patients with AA. With that being said, Nektar intends to release topline data from a 24-week off-treatment period from this REZOLVE-AA study in Q4 of 2026. This might be important, because it could possibly indicate if the drug continues to work on its own with no further dosing.

Financials

According to the 10-K SEC filing, Nektar Therapeutics had cash and investments in marketable securities of $245.8 million as of December 31, 2025. The truth is that this was not sufficient working capital to keep its operations funded for an extended period of time. Why was this the case? That's because according to the 10-K SEC filing, it believed that it only had enough cash on hand to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months. On the flip side, the amount of cash I laid out above doesn't include two financial transactions that were done after the fact. That is, it was able to raise additional cash, which should allow it to fund its operations for an extended period of time.

On November 12, 2025, it entered into a November 2025 ATM Sales Agreement with Piper Sandler & Co. and BTIG, LLC, where it could offer and sell up to an aggregate of $110 million. From February 2026 through March 11, 2026, it tapped into this financing agreement. It sold 639,131 shares of common stock at a price of $71.15 per share in order to raise total net proceeds of $44.1 million. The second financial transaction also came after Q4 2025, ending December 31, 2025. This was a 2026 public offering done on February 13, 2026, whereby it raised a total of $432 million after expenses. The company burns a total of $40.9 million per quarter. This is broken down into $29.7 million in R&D expenses and then $11.2 million in G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Nektar Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the positive data that the company just obtained from the phase 2b REZOLVE-AA study using REZPEG to treat patients with AA. The company intends to release these results at an upcoming medical conference presentation in 2026. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the stock market is going to react positively to this very same data and allow the stock price to trade higher. Secondly, there is no guarantee that the results achieved from this study [as shown above] will be replicated again in a phase 3 clinical trial. Lastly, the intention is to release 24-week off-treatment data from this study. Even though the company showed that clinical activity continued when patients were still given twice-monthly REZPEG doses for another 16 weeks, there is no guarantee that the drug will continue to do well in patients when they are no longer receiving treatment. Such data is expected in Q4 of 2026, and failure to do well here could result in the stock price trading lower.

The second risk to consider would be that of the ongoing development of REZPEG for the treatment of patients with AD. The intention is to initiate the phase 3 ZENITH-AD program using this drug candidate to treat these moderate-to-severe patients in Q2 of 2026. The risk here is that even though all dose arms met the primary endpoint and secondary endpoints [like EASI 75] of the phase 2b REZOLVE-AD study in a statistically significant manner, there is no assurance that all of this clinical data is going to be replicated in the phase 3 ZENITH-AD program, for starters. Secondly, it is the intention of Nektar Therapeutics to release topline 52-week off-treatment data from the phase 2b REZOLVE-AD study in Q1 of 2027. There is no assurance that this data is going to end up being positive, nor that the stock market is going to view this in a highly favorable manner.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the possible expansion opportunity that this company has. The beauty of its IL-2 receptor agonist is that it can also be applied for the treatment of patients with T1D. There is potential for REZPEG in this patient population to help by reducing T-cell and cytokine activity that causes the destruction of insulin-producing pancreatic beta cells. Data from a TrialNet-sponsored phase 2 study using this drug in stage 3 new-onseet T1D patients is expected to be released in 2027. The risk here is that even though REZPEG has shown clinical activity in being able to help treat patients with AD and AA, there is no assurance that it will be able to prevent disease progression in T1D patients.

Conclusion

Nektar Therapeutics has come a long way since I last wrote about it. Its stock has increased by 915.71% since I last mentioned it. This was thanks to the fact that the company was indeed able to establish clinical POC in being able to help treat patients with AD and AA by using Tregs for immune homeostasis and prevention of autoimmune disorders. This was done so in both the phase 2b REZOLVE-AD and REZOLVE-AA trials, respectively. The latest data released from the REZOLVE-AA study provides further proof that the use of Tregs is ideal in treating T-cell-mediated immune disorders.

This was the reason for upgrading the rating from a "Buy" to a "Strong Buy." However, another important reason to do so was because the company has several catalysts that are rapidly approaching. Many of which, I believe, could possibly allow for further upside in the share price. With the positive, blinded 16-week treatment extension period data from the phase 2b REZOLVE-AA study using REZPEG to treat patients with AA, plus the expectation of the initiation of the phase 3 ZENITH-AD program using this drug to treat patients with AD, I believe that this stock rating should be brought up to a "Strong Buy."

data.title: TNGY: Neither Yield Nor Cyclical Edge, Not A Buy Yet
data.description:
There are many energy funds in the market, each with a meaningful overlap with each other; however, they still differ slightly in one or the other aspect and are suited to different investor requirements according to their asset allocation frameworks. Tortoise Energy Fund ETF (TNGY) is a recent addition to energy-focused ETFs. However, TNGY differs meaningfully from other energy-focused funds, with reference to its structuring, its portfolio allocations, its active investment mandate, and so on. This is a successor to the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure and Income Fund (INFIX), which operated as a mutual fund before converting to an ETF in June 2025.

TNGY is structured as a Regulated Investment Company (RIC) and therefore tends to avoid double taxation, which typically occurs in the case of C-Corp funds. At present, the SEC yield of the fund stands at ~2.47%; however, the distribution yield of the fund is higher than the SEC yield at approximately ~4.5%. The trailing quarterly distribution is ~$0.1172, and the fund is currently traded at $9.78, identical to its net asset value per share. Speaking further on the quality of distributions, the last FY annual report suggests that over ~55% of distributions had been attributed to return of capital and not from the returns generated by the fund.

This again reflects the quality of distributions made by TNGY and its preceding fund at present to be inadequate and unconvincing. This assertion is further reinforced by the fact that the fund's stated focus is on maximizing income, with its objective being to maximize current income, with capital appreciation being a secondary aspect. The current income yield does not, however, appear to align well with the said objective, even when that is in fact over the SEC Yield, which does not appear to be a favorable indicator as an investment proposition for an ETF fund.

Performance Evaluation

Although the fund has a launch date of June 2025, TNGY is the successor of the Tortoise Energy Infrastructure and Income Fund (INFIX), and as a result, we have a longer history to evaluate performance. With reference to the last ten-year performance, the fund has been a rather poor capital allocator. Whether it’s the recent time period or since inception, the fund has significantly underperformed its benchmarks, which include the S&P 500 (as cited by the fund, though not necessarily an appropriate benchmark given its mandate). However, over recent years, a slight mean reversion is visible, with the fund at times exceeding the performance of the S&P 500.

However, we should note that recently most energy-focused funds have performed well, with many of them significantly exceeding the performance of the S&P 500. Therefore, I would not be placing significant emphasis on recent performance. Across the cycle, the fund has not demonstrated consistent outperformance. Since it might be said that S&P 500 is not an appropriate benchmark for TNGY, an assertion that seems to be correct as well, I have also tried to evaluate how the fund has performed post its new launch versus some other energy-related indexes.

The reason to consider the ALPS Alerian Energy Infrastructure ETF (ENFR) was due to its exposure to midstream energy infrastructure. The performance of ENFR has materially exceeded that of TNGY. Even one of Tortoise’s own indices, which is the Tortoise North American Pipeline Fund ETF (TPYP), has performed relatively better than TNGY. Of course, these are not perfectly comparable benchmarks for TNGY, but they still suggest that the fund, even on a relative basis, at least in terms of performance, has not been entirely satisfactory. The State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE), which is more focused on the broader energy spectrum but is also heavily weighted toward upstream players, has tended to benefit from recent energy price spikes and has significantly outperformed midstream infrastructure indices, along with the S&P 500 (SPY). To put it in a nutshell, I would keep the performance comparison as neutral to slightly negative for the time being and proceed to evaluate the current portfolio exposure to assess forward expectations and how well it might fit into an investor’s portfolio.

Portfolio Allocation

At present, the fund reflects the nature of the composition to have a good amount of dispersion. While the nature of the fund remains non-diversified, still yet, when it comes to sectors, the fund effectively is exposed to many varied factors. For example, unlike upstream-dominated energy companies, which tend to do well with the rise in commodity prices but fail to perform in case of cyclical downturns, TNGY is also allocated to refining, which is significantly less correlated with energy price fluctuations and more towards crack spreads, or infrastructure, which functions more as a toll booth kind of business model wherein the companies receiving those services tend to pay contractually agreed payments, which again are not solely determined by energy prices; on the contrary, oil & gas prices only play a very small part in the same.

This assertion is also supported by the current holding composition for the fund. Kindly remember that TNGY is an active fund, and therefore security holdings keep on changing. In addition, both the sector allocation and even the asset allocation of the fund are actively managed by Tortoise Capital and keep on changing. Therefore, the present snapshot of holdings is only worth referencing to get a rough view of typically how the fund allocates its assets and not to infer the relative attractiveness or unattractiveness on the basis of current holdings. At present, the primary exposure is predominantly equity, and only a small portion is allocated towards credit/fixed income opportunities, which for now does not warrant a separate mention or discussion.

TNGY is also reported to use select covered call strategies as a yield enhancement; however, that portion again remains quite small and would not materially impact the assessment of the fund. Since it would be very difficult to get real-time data of the options book of the fund, I have looked into their latest annual report, where the premium income received from options was around ~$1.18 million, which, on a total net asset value of ~$500 million, would be a nominal ~0.2%.

Overview Of Investment Manager

This again is an interesting aspect of the fund under current discussion and therefore warrants a separate mention. See, in any of the actively managed funds, the track record and background of portfolio managers are very important. In the case of many ETFs, we do not consider them because of their overlapping nature with their benchmarks and their primary performance being driven by industry tailwinds and developments. In the case of TNGY, however, the case is a little different. Tortoise Capital has been in business for over ~20 years, and they still manage their core operations with a four-person investment team. These are the people who analyze and make buy and sell decisions for the fund. Tortoise Capital has other ETFs as well apart from TNGY and manages cumulatively over $10 billion of AUM. However, all of the ETFs are focused and related to the energy sector only. The portfolio managers consider it to be their core expertise and therefore a focus area.

Taking into account that this same four-person team has run Tortoise’s flagship energy strategies together for over a decade and TNGY could be considered more of a restructuring and refining of an already existing mandate, a payment of ~0.85% appears to be reasonable. For the active nature of the fund scouting for opportunities across the energy spectrum, investors are getting a good value proposition in terms of expenses paid for that service.

Expectation And Opinion On TNGY

It is not much of a doubt that the demand for energy infrastructure would tend to increase meaningfully over the next decade. Take the example of data centers; energy infrastructure is critical for their development. Instances where data centers could not be constructed because they were denied energy access have come to light in a few cases. Data centers are just one example; many other types of infrastructural development rely heavily on energy consumption, which in turn depends on energy infrastructure such as pipelines, for example, for the effective transmission of energy from producers to end consumers. The fact is that it is not easy to build energy infrastructure. It is a CapEx-heavy development, contingent on a lot of approvals, which creates capacity constraints. It is not that a new player would come and develop an energy pipeline next year. You can have a company developing a revolutionary AI technology over a year, but not a natural gas pipeline; that’s how asset-heavy development works. Therefore, it would not be incorrect to assess that there are reasonable tailwinds at present for the energy value chain.

TNGY, by exposure to companies and opportunities in the space, both equity and credit, along with an experienced portfolio management team, naturally stands to be in an advantageous position to capture some of the incremental returns that active allocation might provide. However, considering the limited historical performance of TNGY along with its relatively recent launch, I would want to keep the fund on a watchlist for now instead of directly committing. Further, TNGY neither offers any hedge against market shocks caused by energy price spikes nor tends to be materially benefited by those price uptrends, as is the case with upstream oil and gas producers and the ETFs primarily exposed to them. In addition, those upstream-heavy energy funds are also suitable for tactical exposure or to trade on a near-term view on energy prices, whether as a speculative proposition, dynamic asset allocation, or a core portfolio hedge. In my opinion, TNGY does not fit into either, and therefore it is kept on hold for now.

An argument could also be made that TNGY in substance might be inclined more towards a fixed income-like profile or a hybrid of equity and fixed income, and therefore it might be analyzed from that perspective. However, I would not want to do that when the present distribution yield is around ~4%, close to risk-free rates, with the current SEC yield being even lower at ~2%. A significant portion of distributions being in the form of return of capital was also already highlighted at the start.

Limitations To Hold Case

Since TNGY has been restructured from its predecessor fund, we do not really have an adequate performance history to reasonably conclude and make an assertion on performance. It is possible that the fund with its new launch might improve on the performance of its predecessor fund and improve its track record. The energy infrastructure tailwind is intact, and the wide mandate to invest across the energy value chain and asset classes gives the ETF a much-needed advantage to capitalize on opportunities that might emerge in the near term. The same proposition, however, is not guaranteed and can take numerous turns, many of which can upset the case for the companies even if those developments take place. Commodity price exposure cannot be totally excluded either and would eventually impact the economics of underlying companies in which TNGY allocates the capital.

Further, active allocation results in higher costs relative to passive allocation, both at the portfolio level and investor level. The fund, therefore, if it keeps underperforming, may leave investors disappointed. Further, even though the fund suggests being energy-focused, unlike many energy-focused funds, it does not stand to benefit materially from energy uptrends, a fact that investors should keep in mind. In addition, the quality of distributions remains a consideration, as portions of payouts may not be fully supported by underlying income, which investors again should be evaluated and monitored carefully. Also, as highlighted earlier, even a hold is not justified if the objective is for a tactical exposure to benefit from energy upcycles.

In Conclusion

TNGY at an initial glance seems to be an interesting allocation for an income-oriented investor, with a fund having a wide mandate to invest across the energy value chain. However, the current SEC yield of ~2.47% and distribution yield of ~4.78% do not seem to do complete justice to the said objective, that of being primarily to generate current income, as also stated by the fund. Also, the fund further does not benefit to a similar level that other energy-focused funds such as XLE or PEO might gain with reference to continued energy price uptrends and therefore loses its relative appeal both as a case of benefiting from energy uptrends and as a tactical exposure to an energy cycle, either as a speculative proposition or as a core portfolio hedge. Therefore, it is rated as a hold at present.

data.title: Gilead Sciences: Why I'm Tempted To Sell Ahead Of Q1 Earnings - But Won't
data.description:
Investment Overview - Background To Q1 Earnings & M&A Spree

Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD), the Foster City, California-based Pharma giant, is set to announce its Q1 earnings on 23rd April - this Thursday. Analysts' consensus revenue forecast is ~$6.92bn, with GAAP earnings per share ("EPS") of $1.59, and normalized EPS of $1.91.

Covering Gilead in a note for Seeking Alpha in November last year, I wrote:

In May 2024, Gilead stock was worth ~$65 per share, so it has risen ~100% in 18 months, and the upside has been more or less constant. In my view, this may be due to the company learning the lessons from several misguided M&A deals that attempted to kick-start its oncology division, and the unstoppable growth of its HIV division.

Since 2018, the HIV division has doubled in size, from $9.1bn per annum, to $19.6bn in 2024, and I have conservatively estimated $19.94bn for 2025. Sales in Q3 of $5.3bn were up 4% year-on-year, led by the "mega-blockbuster" Biktarvy, the world's fourth best-selling prescription drug in 2025, by my calculation.

My forecast was conservative, because Gilead's HIV division earned ~$20.7bn, or 70% of the company's total revenues in 2025, led by the HIV-1 integrase blocker Biktarvy - revenues of $14.3bn - and supported by the launch of Yeztugo, (lenacapavir), a capsid inhibitor and twice yearly administered HIV prevention med whose peak sales have been forecast at ~$4.5bn. In 2026, Gilead forecasts Yeztugo revenues of $800m, and HIV division revenue growth of ~6%.

As mentioned, however, oncology has been something of an Achilles Heel for Gilead - in 2025, the division revenues fell by 2% year-on-year, to $3.24bn, with only Trodelvy, acquired via Gilead's $21bn acquisition of Immunomedics in 2020, showing growth of 6% year-on-year, to $1.4bn of revenues.

The only other two approved drugs in the division, the cell therapies (indicated for heme cancers) Yescarta and Tecartus, earned a combined $1.84bn last year, however Gilead has forecasted a ~10% decline in their revenues in 2026 due to competitive threats.

In 2026 to date, Gilead has attempted to address this imbalance by embarking on an M&A spree - the Pharma has spent $3.15bn to acquire German Pharma Tubulis, and its antibody drug conjugate ("ADC", the same mechanism of action as Trodelvy) TUB-040, indicated for ovarian cancer, $1.675bn to acquire Ouro and its anti-inflammatory drug candidate OM336 (gamgertamig), and $7.8bn acquiring Arcellx, and its BCMA CAR-T cell therapy anito-cel, indicated for relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma.

None of these products will earn any commercial revenues for Gilead in 2026, although anito-cel may secure approval in December of this year, and Gilead has indicated in a press release that any in-process research and development ("IPR&D") impact from these acquisitions will not be included in Q1 earnings, rather in Q2, although the company does note that:

For the first quarter of 2026, Gilead currently expects ~$107M in acquired IPR&D expenses, representing a charge of ~$0.07 to both GAAP and non-GAAP diluted earnings per share.

The first quarter of 2026 acquired IPR&D expenses includes the ~$80M upfront payment related to the Genhouse collaboration announced on February 13, 2026.

The Genhouse collaboration involves Gilead securing the global rights to China based Pharma Genhouse's MAT2A-targetting synthetic lethal therapy - another oncology drug.

Big M&A Bets Will Test Management's Caliber Given Past Failures

I have made the point in prior notes that Gilead's CEO Daniel O'Day was formerly Chief Executive Officer of Roche (RHHBY) Pharmaceuticals - a company whose oncology division is its largest by revenue generated, $29.8bn in 2025, or 38% of its total revenues - but that the CEO had struggled to ignite Gilead's own oncology division.

Besides the Immunomedics acquisition, Gilead has completed the $4.9bn buyout of Forty Seven, and paid ~$12bn to acquire Kite Pharma - in that context, revenues of <$4bn in 2025 seems a disappointing return on investment.

Today, news has broken that Gilead has discontinued a study of its Arcus Biosciences (RCUS) partnered anti-TIGIT therapy domvanalimab in lung cancer after a futility analysis - according to Seeking Alpha:

STAR-121 evaluated domvanalimab with chemotherapy and anti-PD-1 antibody zimberelimab as a first-line option for metastatic non-small cell lung cancer versus chemotherapy and pembrolizumab, an anti-PD-1 drug Merck (MRK) markets as Keytruda.

While the IDRC’s review didn’t include safety data, its regular reviews didn’t identify any safety concerns related to domvanalimab, Arcus (RCUS) said.

Concurrently, the company announced the discontinuation of its Phase 2 EDGE-Lung study, which evaluated domvanalimab with various immunotherapies in patients with lung cancer.

Another failure to digest, then, but clearly Gilead continues to prioritize its oncology division, investing nearly $11bn acquiring two new companies - will their new candidates succeed where so many others have failed?

An overview of the Tubulis acquisition is provided below (source: Gilead presentation):

Olaparib, brand name Lynparza, a poly ADP-ribose polymerase ("PARP") inhibitor approved to treat ovarian, breast and pancreatic cancer and marketed and sold by AstraZeneca (AZN), earned revenues of ~$3.3bn last year. Corcept Therapeutics (CORT) relacorilant, brand name Lifyorli, was approved in March, also to treat patients with platinum-resistant epithelial ovarian ​cancer, with a peak revenue estimate of ~$550m. This highlights the fact that Ovarian is a large market, but also that it is a competitive market.

On a recent call to discuss the acquisition of Tubulis, Gilead's Chief Medical Officer Dietmar Berger, MD, PhD, appointed in January last year, told analysts:

Building on our experience with Trodelvy and years of diligence, we first partnered with Tubulis in 2024 to apply their ADC platform to a Gilead provided antibody with an undisclosed target. This Gilead-owned ADC is currently in preclinical development. Our experience working with the Tubulis platform has provided us with great insights into the platform's capabilities. In addition, the compelling clinical data that Tubulis has recently shared have increased our confidence that Tubulis has the right technology to meet our aspirations.

TUB-040 targets NaPi2b, which is expressed in approximately 90% of ovarian cancer cells while having minimal expression in normal cells. This allows TUB-040 to deliver extremely potent tumor killing payloads directly to the ovarian cancer cells expressing NaPi2b and still spare healthy cells to avoid toxicity.

The 50% response rate achieved in clinical studies may be impressive, but TUB-040 will not enter a registrational Phase 3 study until 2027, management warns, hence this particular deal may not result in any commercial revenue before the end of the decade.

As mentioned, Gilead anticipates that anito-cel will be approved in December of this year. The Pharma paid a >65% premium to Arcellx' traded share price to make this deal, and will hope to challenge the likes of Legend Biotech (LEGN) and Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) Carvykti in multiple myeloma ("MM") markets. Its cell therapy subsidiary Kite had already been working with Arcellx for several years, and there is a belief that, while comparable to Carvykti on efficacy, anito-cel may have the edge when it comes to safety.

On its recent call Gilead's Vice President of Kite, Cindy Prettie, told analysts:

Beyond Anito-cel, the acquisition of Arcellx earlier-stage clinical programs, in addition to differentiated and scalable technology platform built on the proprietary D-Domain binders. These binders have the potential to expand our pipeline and research efforts to next-generation autologous CAR Ts, in vivo CAR Ts as well as T-cell engagers over time. Following the acquisition, we will capture the full economics of Anito-cel sales by eliminating cost sharing and royalties, as well as up to $1.5 billion in anticipated regulatory and development milestone payments.

In summary, these two oncology M&A deals strike me as following the same blueprint as previous M&A deals - no immediate revenues, and lots of work still required to establish a competitive position in highly competitive markets.

For example, just yesterday, Eli Lilly (LLY), the world's most valuable Pharma company, paid ~$7bn to acquire privately held Kelonia Therapeutics, whose lead asset is apparently "KLN-1010, a one-time CAR-T cell therapy designed to target a blood cancer known as multiple myeloma."

Meanwhile, Gilead's R&D efforts in the field of immunology have not been especially impressive, either. Jyseleca, a janus kinase inhibitor, has not yet secured approval in the U.S., while a program evaluating two assets, cilofexor and firsocostat, in metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (“MASH”) was discontinued last year. Gilead's CMO says of the Ouro deal:

Ouro Medicine's lead asset, OM336 or gamgertamig, is a clinical stage BCMAxCD3 bispecific T-cell engager with potentially superior potency and lower CRS than other BCMA T-cell engagers.

In addition, OM336 is administered subcutaneously and has shown clinical proof of concept in approximately 60 patients across immune-mediated diseases. OM336 is an ongoing development for multiple orphan autoimmune indications with high unmet need, including autoimmune hemolytic anemia, immune thrombocytopenia, pemphigus vulgaris, pemphigus foliaceus and idiopathic inflammatory myopathies.

Once again, however, a pivotal study of the drug is not expected to initiate until 2027, at the earliest. As promising a field as T-cell engagers is, it is right to factor in a significant prospect that OM336 ultimately falls short of best-in-class status.

What To Expect When Results Are Announced This Thursday?

Turning to more immediate news, will Gilead stock continue its impressive bull run when Q1 earnings are announced on Thursday?

As mentioned, stock is up >100% on a five year basis, and on a three-year basis, among the world's largest U.S. and European Pharmas, only Eli Lilly and Novartis (NVS) have achieved a better rate of share price growth than Gilead stock's 73% gain.

That is arguably surprising, given that Gilead's five-year revenue CAGR between 2020 - 2025 is <5%, one of the lowest rates of growth among global Pharmas. Guidance for 2026 was shared in Gilead's 2025 earnings presentation as follows:

Gilead earned $29.44bn of revenues in 2025, so the growth forecast for 2026 is negligible, partly due to the lack of a revenue contribution from COVID med Veklury this year - in 2024 and 2025 Veklury earned $1.8bn, and $911m.

In the table above I have shared some historical and projected data for Gilead's product portfolio, and as we can see, I speculate that Gilead's HIV products Biktarvy and Descovy will earn almost 60% of the Pharma's total revenues, with Yeztugo beginning to make a greater contribution to overall revenues. As expected, I have Tecartus and Yescarta revenues projected to fall, but for Trodelvy revenues to keep growing at a rate of ~25% per annum.

I think this may suggest that Gilead has a potential over-reliance on its HIV division - its major competitor in this field is GSK (GSK), whose HIV division earned revenues of nearly $8bn last year, and whose drug Apretude represents the main challenger to Yeztugo. Apretude is administered as a single intramuscular injection, while Yeztugo is given via two subcutaneous injections, which may suggest Apretude has a competitive advantage.

As such, ahead of Gilead's Q1 earnings, I wonder if there a case for selling the stock? I have held GILD stock for around 3 years, and it is one of my better investments, having doubled in value, but there are some red flags to consider ahead of Q1 earnings. The IPR&D hit may be minor, but we know there will be a much bigger hit in Q2. Growth will be somewhat sluggish, and while, like all Pharma companies, Gilead is a highly profitable company, achieving a net profit margin of nearly 30% last year, EPS is likely to be similar to 2025 also.

Gilead's CEO has commented that:

Building on our recent consistent track record of commercial, clinical and financial execution, Gilead's portfolio is the most robust and diverse ever. In 2026 alone, we expect 5 Phase III updates across virology, oncology and inflammation, highlighting the productivity of our clinical pipeline. Our pipeline is also driving up to 10 ongoing and potential new launches through 2027 across our 3 therapeutic areas of focus.

It is not unimpressive, but within a highly competitive industry, Gilead's pipeline is not as stacked as many of its rivals, in my view. Of course, management is attempting to address that with its M&A deals, and Gilead is substantially less indebted than key rivals, reporting cash of >$9.6bn as of the end of 2025, with long-term debt a relatively modest ~$22bn, but the reality is that this business cannot match rivals' growth rates with regard to either the top or bottom lines.

Then, if we consider the company's relatively poor M&A track record, it makes me wonder what the market makes of the recent M&A spree? It certainly feels to me as if the deals Gilead has done does not necessarily illustrate that the company has learned from past mistakes, but either way, it may take years to truly discover if the deals done this year will work out.

Concluding Thoughts: Why I'll Likely Keep Holding GILD Despite My Expectation For An Underwhelming Quarter

In recent weeks, Gilead stock's long bull run has lost momentum, and stock has sunk from a mid-February all-time high of >$155 per share, to $136 per share at the time of writing.

As discussed, there is no particular obvious reason (to me at least) why Gilead will show strong outperformance in Q1, and I expect management to be quizzed on its >$11bn outlay on M&A to date in 2026. In short, I am not expecting stock to recover gains lost across the past couple of months on a stronger than expected set of Q1 earnings.

However, if we think about 2026 guidance, we must consider a forward price to sales ratio of ~6x, and a forward price to earnings ratio of ~16x. I'd speculate that is about average regarding the P/S, but below average regarding P/E, i.e., superior to most rival Pharmas. A very good reason to keep holding stock.

When evaluating investment opportunities, it is often tempting to focus on weaknesses rather than strengths, and with regard to Gilead, I believe it is particularly easy to fall into that trap, i.e., to conclude that without its HIV division the company looks vulnerable.

The reality is however that Gilead is globally dominant in this field, which also happens to be a highly lucrative field. Its HIV division earns more than twice as much as its biggest rival, its key products have >50% market share, and the earliest patent expiry - for Biktarvy - does not occur until 2036.

In summary, then, Gilead's HIV division is (to my mind) almost entirely responsible for its recent share price growth, and, GSK aside, it is largely unthreatened by rivals, patent expiries, and even price controls - like most Pharmas, Gilead has struck a deal with the Trump administration, pledging to invest >$30bn in U.S. manufacturing, meaning it does not have to worry too much about being subject to "most favored nation" pricing in its largest market.

The jury may be very much out on Gilead's recent M&A spree, given its poor track record, but we must remember that developing "blockbuster" (>$1bn per annum) drugs is an expensive business characterized by a high failure rate. In that context, the fact that Gilead markets and sells what was globally the ninth best-selling drug in 2025 (my research suggests), in Biktarvy, provides significant comfort for anyone investing in Gilead.

I am therefore not thinking of selling my Gilead stock ahead of Q1 earnings, despite some potential Sell signals. Instead I will continue to hold, and collect a generous dividend yielding >2% per annum.

I'll also be hoping, however, that Gilead can finally establish itself as a bona fide oncology player - the HIV division papers over the cracks created by a series of underwhelming M&A deals in recent years (although the $4.3bn spent acquiring Cyma Bay in 2022 has been a rare success - I expect liver disease drug Lizdelvi to break $500m in revenues this year), and this time around, management must show that it can convert promise into reality. If it cannot, it's possible I could live to regret not selling my stock when it reached >$150 per share earlier this year.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: OVL: Strong Performance, Now Strong 10.5% Distribution Rate
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The Overlay Shares Large Cap Equity ETF (OVL) is not a new ETF offering, going back to a launch in 2019. However, the fund has never received too much attention. The fund sponsor is fairly small and niche—that can tend to make it a harder uphill battle for newer investment advisors. They do have some history, though, not only in OVL but also since they were founded in 2013.

Further, despite what we'll see as solid performance out of OVL, the fund's distribution wasn't that enticing to income-focused investors. Since switching to a target distribution rate and monthly payout schedule, this likely should generate more interest from investors. They have several funds, and this also applies to their Overlay Shares Small Cap Equity ETF (OVS) and Overlay Shares Foreign Equity ETF (OVF). However, we'll be sticking to covering just OVL today.

With that said, the strong track record plus a more income-focused strategy make this all a more enticing investment choice for investors considering something a bit differentiated for their portfolio. That can make it an interesting choice for investors today.

OVL Basics

  • Dividend Frequency: Monthly
  • Dividend Yield: 10.49%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.79%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $166.158 million
  • Structure: Active ETF

OVL's investment objective is to "seek to outperform the S&P 500 TR Index through a combination of capital appreciation and income generation." To do that, the fund provides "large cap passive equity exposure with active risk-managed put selling option overlay that seeks to generate additional income."

More specifically, when looking at the fund's holdings, they'll be employing put spread selling rather than short put positions alone. That caps some of the income generation in terms of the premiums collected on the short puts, which go to buy long puts, but it also caps downside moves and provides a more minimal maximum loss scenario.

Technically, when selling short puts, your maximum loss is known ahead of time because it would be if the underlying investment went to zero. While not probable, technically, it is not impossible that the S&P 500 Index goes to zero. The chances of that happening are incredibly minimal, but the chances that it falls 5%-20% happen somewhat regularly as part of normal market gyrations. In other words, the bottom line, the maximum loss, is defined.

As we can see, they are truly going for passive equity exposure here, as they highlighted, in that they only invest in the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO). The credit call spreads are also taking a laddered approach regarding the strike prices selected and the expiration dates. This more staggered approach allows the fund managers the flexibility to tweak their position every couple of weeks, depending on broader conditions.

This is similar to the NEOS strategy when they write their call contracts, which helps to limit the overall upside cap that is inherent in call writing funds. With OVL being focused on put writing, it does change up the risk/reward, but the general strategy of staggered options providing more flexibility is the same.

Strong Historical Results Look Promising

As we would expect, the above-discussed strategy means that OVL is a derivatives income strategy; therefore, Morningstar would categorize it as such. On that front, Morningstar gives this fund between a four and five-star rating, indicating a strong track record of past performance. We see 5 stars as of 2/28/2026 on the fund's website, then 4 stars in the latest fact sheet for Q1 2026. So, it varies but still remains respectable.

However, where most derivative-focused ETFs fall short is against the S&P 500 Index. Generally, when we are talking about derivative ETFs, we often talk about call writing funds. In this case, we know that OVL utilizes put selling instead.

With that said, the fund has been able to outperform on all time frames against the major equity benchmark.

That tends to be quite an impressive feat, not only for a plain, actively managed large-cap fund but also for one that utilizes options to generate relatively higher distribution rates to investors. While this is only past performance and cannot guarantee future results, the track record is at least somewhat impressive considering it is all time frames—not just a certain annualized period that we could draw attention to.

Distribution Policy Change

Starting this year, they've initiated a 10.5% distribution rate policy that is paid monthly, rather than quarterly as it was previously. In prior years, they were taking a more conservative payout approach. Similar to most ETFs, the fund provides a distribution calendar for the entire year ahead of time, meaning investors know exactly when the next ex and payment dates will be.

This new policy can likely make it a more viable income-focused investment instrument for those types of investors. The trailing-twelve-month distribution policy comes in at less than 6%, which includes several of these new payouts. That helps to highlight just how much of an increase the payout will be.

They are also estimating that as of March 30, 2026, 100% of the distribution is "attributable to return of capital." If that remains throughout the year, that can also make it a tax-friendly distribution, as ROC reduces an investor's cost basis. Therefore, it doesn't become potentially taxable until the units are sold.

Looking back historically, the fund has been able to earn over 10.5% in each of the annualized periods of performance. That would indicate that, at least through this period, the fund would have been able to cover this distribution without eroding the NAV/share price.

Of course, that would have come at the sacrifice of seeing the share price also climb as high during this period. They launched around $25, and it has now doubled to nearly $51—even after the latest broader equity market pullback we've seen in the large-cap space. So with this shift, instead of paying out only the income and option premiums generated, they will be dipping into their underlying capital appreciation to fuel the payout.

Conclusion

OVL has delivered strong historical returns, exceeding the S&P 500 Index itself in all standardized timeframes as of the end of March 31, 2026. That doesn't guarantee that it will continue to be able to deliver that going forward, but it is at least a promising track record. Further, the fund has now shifted to a more income-friendly distribution policy that likely makes it a more interesting proposition to those investors. They tend to prefer monthly distributions and higher distribution yields. In this case, the track record suggests that they could cover this level of payout as well.

data.title: Sell In May? 5 Reasons Why
data.description:
Equities surged to all-time highs in trading last week. The NASDAQ rose 6.8% to lead the charge. The tech-heavy index has now seen 13 straight winning sessions, its longest winning streak since 1992. The rally broadened late in the week after the Iranian Foreign Minister announced it would allow passage through the Strait of Hormuz during the current ceasefire. Small caps rallied strongly, with the Russell 2000 climbing more than six percent on the week.

With a week and a half to go in April, is it time to embrace the old investment adage to 'Sell in May?' or are further gains in equities ahead? This article will highlight five things that should give prudent investors pause on whether this rally will continue as we get deeper into Spring.

Iran Isn't Over:

This is probably the most obvious reason to be apprehensive about the weeks ahead. The hopes that the Strait of Hormuz was on its way to being reopened that drove the markets higher last week had the half-life of a mouse fart. The weekend saw Iranian gunships opening fire on an Indian-owned and manned tanker after that ship had gotten permission to transverse this transit point.

Iran’s Revolutionary Guard has now stated the Strait will remain closed until the U.S. lifts its blockade of Iranian ports. The POTUS has simultaneously stated that a broader peace agreement remains within reach while also threatening to blow up every bridge and energy facility in Iran if the Strait is not reopened. The US military is also apparently preparing to board Iran-linked oil tankers and seize commercial ships in international waters.

If this degree of uncertainty persists into this week, a good chunk of last week's gains will be given back. The longer the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, the longer prices for oil, LNG, jet fuel, diesel, phosphate, helium, phosphate, urea, etc., remain elevated. And each week this goes on, the greater the chances of significant damage to the global economy. And without 'boots on the ground', the Strait of Hormuz is near impossible to reopen as long as the Iranian regime is willing to continue to endure despite being pummeled militarily.

Investor Complacency:

Investors have been much too passive about the impacts to inflation from the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which is captured nicely by the chart above. As can be seen, fuel and fertilizer prices have exploded and that is even with huge releases from strategic petroleum reserves in the U.S. and Europe.
