The latter is particularly vulnerable given their dependency on energy exports from the Middle East and the continent is about to experience a major shortage of jet fuel which will lead to a mass cancellation of flights if this situation is not resolved soon. Asia is even more dependent on energy supplies from the region.

And despite this, the S&P 500 is up since four percent since the war began, and market volatility measured by the S&P VIX Index (VIX) has dropped. This divergence is one of the best examples of investor complacency in this market I can highlight. And given this huge surge of prices across myriad energy and commodity products, there is a lot of inflation pressure in the pipeline that will play out in coming months and quarters across the global economy.

Valuations:

With equities being at all-time highs, markets are trading at levels rarely seen in history (notably at the end of the Internet Boom) via various historical metrics.

The major banks like Goldman Sachs (GS), Bank of America (BAC) and Morgan Stanley (MS) kicked off first quarter earnings season last week. Overall, results were better than expected including at JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM). However, good results seem more than priced into this sector as banks rarely trade for more than two times book value. Currently, JP Morgan is trading at over 2.4 times tangible book value.

Credit Markets Deteriorating:

If it wasn't for the problems in the Middle East, investors focus would be on the deteriorating parts of the credit ecosystem. Most notably on private credit. Volatility has spiked since problems first cropped up at Blue Owl Capital (OWL) in the back half of February.

Since then, large private credit funds run by the likes of Morgan Stanley (MS), Apollo Global Management (APO), Cliffwater, Ares Management Corporation (ARES) and others have 'gated' investor redemptions triggering further concerns about the space.

Fitch Ratings pegged the default rate in the private credit sector at a record 9.2% at the end of 2025. This is already higher than the peak corporate credit default rate banks saw during the Great Financial Crisis. UBS has a worse-case private credit default rate for private credit of 15%. It is little wonder that JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) marked down some collateral values and pulled in some credit lines to private credit funds in March.

And investors should not take their eyes the worsening situation around commercial real estate or CRE markets. Some $5 trillion of CRE debt is outstanding, nearly three times the size of the private credit space. Overall Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities or CMBS delinquency rates have broached the 7.5% threshold. It was lower than two percent before the Federal Reserve started to raise rates in March 2022.

Office and multi-family accounts for roughly 70% of CRE debt outstanding. CMBS delinquency rates are near 12%, higher than the peak of the GFC. Multi-Family CMBS delinquency rates have roughly doubled to over 7% over the past five quarters.

Mid-Terms Approaching:

Finally, we have this interesting data point. Since 2008, the S&P 500 has only two down years. Those were in 2018 (-4.38%) and 2022 (-18.11%), which happened to be the last two mid-term years. With the current administration holding razor thin margins in the House, and a 53-47 majority in the Senate; the most likely outcome in November is at least one component of congress will flip. This will bring additional uncertainty around legislative priorities in the two years ahead. And it should be noted that the current congressional configuration has already resulted in the longest government shutdowns in U.S. history.

Conclusion:

Given this outlook, my portfolio remains conservatively positioned. With last Friday's option expiration date and thanks to some covered call holdings expiring in the money, roughly 30% of my portfolio is in short term treasuries and cash. The rest is largely in covered call positions around the few good value and GARP stocks I am finding within the current market.

data.title: Eli Lilly: We Haven't Reached Its Peak Yet (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Since my last Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) analysis, the stock has declined by 12.5% in price. At the time, I issued a Hold rating after trimming my position by 50% for a 39% return as a short-term tactical trade. Since then, I did exit completely for strong gains. Now, with the time-value increasing amid a lower stock price, I'm returning to a bullish stance and eyeing up a stock fair sentiment value of about $1,150 in 12 months. The current price is ~$925, so the implied fair-value upside is 24%. This implies a moderately strong near-term return trajectory that is further supported by a robust medium-term fundamental growth outlook.

Eli Lilly is definitively in agreement to acquire Kelonia, and Lilly has Q1 2026 earnings approaching on 4/30/2026 during market hours. These are two catalysts that could promote upside in the stock price, making right now look like a good entry point in hindsight.

Kelonia Acquisition

A >$2B strategic acquisition of Kelonia will be strategically sensible for Lilly, but it's important to gain perspective that the near-term financial impact would be minimal. Such a deal would largely sit in the same playbook as Orna and Ventyx deals recently, using the obesity windfall to buy platform optionality and diversify beyond incretins while protecting the premium valuation multiple.

Kelonia's lead asset, called KLN-1010, is a Phase 1 in vivo anti-BCMA CAR-T for multiple myeloma. Even though the early human data were encouraging, the dataset at this time is still tiny and the modality is still young. This means that the >$2B acquisition will be paying for platform scarcity and future optionality instead of de-risked oncology revenue.

Kelonia does already have strategic ties, though, as it signed an in vivo CAR-T collaboration with J&J (JNJ) in 2025 and an earlier Astellas/Xyphos research-license deal covering two programs. Lilly investors focusing on the fine details will want clean answers on what rights, targets, and economics it will actually control.

Q1 2026 Earnings Preview

With April 30 just 10 days away as I write this, I have enough reason to believe the event should be a positive read-through for LLY investors. Kelonia, if it happens, will be a Q2 capital allocation focus, not a Q1 operating condition.

Eli Lilly's broader engine is still massive, as in Q4 2025, Mounjaro generated $7.41B and Zepbound $4.26B. For FY25 they totaled $36.5B, and those two products made up 56% of 2025 revenue. The market leadership and technological moat in these products explain the company's premium valuation multiple, but it also makes the stock a concentrated bet on obesity-management leadership.

One of the most important aspects of April 30 will be whether management reaffirms, or (better) raises, the $80B to $83B 2026 revenue guide and the $33.50 to $35.00 non-GAAP EPS range.

Foundayo, which is Eli Lilly's oral GLP-1 receptor agonist (same class as Ozempic/Wegovy but a pill) and has the generic name orforglipron, is LLY's next bull-leg catalyst. The drug was FDA approved on April 1, and Lilly began shipping on April 6. Management is planning to submit it for type 2 diabetes by the end of Q2 after new phase 3 safety and cardiovascular data. The upside catalyst here is not only the potential 2026 sales of $1.5B to $2.8B, but also the fact that the brand has the potential to deliver multiple labels from one oral asset.

Lilly is also widening access to its consumer-patient portfolio with targeted efficiency through LillyDirect, employer channels, lower-cost Zepbound cash-pay options, low entry prices for Foundayo, and discounted obesity access for Medicare beneficiaries by July 1.

That means we're looking at bullish volume, and only about 1 in 10 who are overweight or obese are currently using GLP-1s. With Lilly's CEO David Ricks saying that weight-loss drugs may reach about 50% of the overweight and obese people who could benefit from them, that means we're well from the peak yet. As a long-term investor, I'd stay in LLY, and as a short-term trader, I view this as a suitable sentiment-value entry point for a quick re-rating return on looming Foundayo bullishness.

Valuation

I've drawn a very simple trendline on the chart below, which works well for me when I consider the stability of medium-term fundamental growth, which should create continued upside and multiple normalization. The trendline implies a $1,150 stock price in 12 months, which is about 24% upside from present. That said, given the way that the markets tend to overshoot ahead of fair value, it's plausible this return could materialize in six months or less.

LLY is probably best valued based on growth-adjusted price-to-fundamentals, because its forward non-GAAP PEG is -27% below the sector median while its forward P/E non-GAAP is 48% higher than the sector median. This points to a higher absolute valuation due to the fact that earnings growth is so high, allowing the market to reasonably price the equity higher without entering speculative territory.

Consensus is important to gauge sentiment. Eli Lilly's consensus EPS estimates from analysts show +43% year-over-year growth in Fiscal 2026, +21.6% in Fiscal 2027, and +16.3% in Fiscal 2028. Those are diminishing returns, but they are stable, strong, and protected by a wide market leadership and technological moat.

My personal estimate is April 2028 normalized EPS of $47.50, with a forward P/E of 24 in April 2027, leading to a reasonable estimate of ~$1,150 in 12 months in the base case.

Under-Appreciated Catalysts

The gray market is a quiet tailwind right now, as the FDA has said that compounders can only copy drugs if they remain on the shortage list. Also, Lilly highlighted impurities in compounded tirzepatide products. Branded supply is normalizing, so the copy market will likely take a back foot from here.

Supply confidence is strengthened by new capacity, including a >$3.5B Pennsylvania site for next-gen obesity drugs such as retatrutide. Lilly's 10-K details a broad U.S. and European buildout. 2027-2031 supply confidence is therefore strengthened. That said, be aware that capex costs can limit near-term margin upside as the cost of long-term structural operational security.

Retatrutide is also an under-appreciated upside lever, as management reported in March that Phase 3 diabetes data showed A1C reductions of between 1.7% and 2% and up to 16.8% weight loss in 40 weeks. If the coming data holds up, Lilly could have another major franchise to layer on top of tirzepatide and Foundayo.

Risks

One of the primary risks I've noticed with the LLY investment case relates to total addressable market, or TAM, durability. In a study of 126,984 adults by JAMA Network Open, 12-month persistence on any GLP-1RA was 24.5%. That means that less than 1 in 4 stayed on therapy after a year. This creates somewhat of a half-life to demand that could deter serious long-term investors very quickly after growth rates in adoption begin to show signs of an impending plateau.

Also, Foundayo has not yet been fully de-risked as an asset yet. The FDA required postmarketing work on MACE, DILI, delayed gastric aspiration, and lactation exposure. Some reports are due through 2029, so there is the potential for volatility here if real-world tolerability or label perception proves choppier than currently indicated.

Conclusion: Buy

I like the setup of LLY stock at this juncture. The valuation looks rich in absolute terms, but relative to the underlying earnings growth, the entry point looks strong indeed. I believe the company has much more to give on a fundamental basis, even if we have reached a point of somewhat diminishing returns already. This is later-stage than I like in a growth vector, but there's still much more upside in the works, and I'm confident in my Buy rating as a result.

Both my simplified technical analysis and fundamentally-oriented price target reach a forecast of about $1,150 per share in 12 months as a fair value. I would not be surprised if the price hits this in six months or less, but if that were the case, I'd exit or trim rather than hold without action.

data.title: RYLD: Selling The Wrong Strategy For The Small Cap Rotation
data.description:
The Global X Russell 2000 Covered Call ETF (RYLD) is a passively managed ETF that follows the CBOE Russell 2000 BuyWrite Index (BXR).

The fund holds Russell 2000 exposure through the Global X Russell 2000 ETF and writes ATM 1-month to expiry call options on the Russell 2000 Index (RUT) against the entire underlying position.

I am initiating RYLD as a Sell on Seeking Alpha. The single most concerning point in the entire fund profile sits on the fund page and is concerning. The most pressing issue is that as of April 17, 2026, the fund has exactly 0.29% of upside before the short call cap hits. The Russell 2000 is at 2,776.90, and the May 15 short call strike is 2,785. 28 days, 29 bps of room. Any small-cap rally from here is effectively sold to the call buyer. The distribution yield of 11.62% looks compelling, but the 30-day SEC yield sits at 1.56%, and that gap is funded by return of capital. With the Russell 2000 up approx. 12% YTD and broadening rotation already in motion, RYLD caps the exact upside that small-cap investors are trying to capture right now.

RYLD Strategy

From the options details section of the fund page, updated at market close on 04/17/2026:

Option Position Notional Exposure Strike Upside Before Cap Expiration Calendar Days to Expiry
Russell 2000 Call Written Contract $1,324,581,300 2,785 0.29% May 15, 2026 28

Notional value on the short call is approx. $1.32 billion, against $1.31 billion in net assets. The fund has written calls on ~100% of NAV. The strike is 2,785, the index is at 2,776.90, and the call expires May 15. If the Russell 2000 moves up more than 29 basis points over the next 28 calendar days, every dollar of upside past that point belongs to the call buyer, not the RYLD shareholder.

This is how this fund and other derivative income funds that utilize ATM calls in addition to tracking an index work. RYLD writes one-month ATM call options on the Russell 2000 Index, rolling on the third Friday of each month at the standard monthly index option expiration. Each new call is struck at or slightly above spot at the time of the write, giving the fund approximately 30 calendar days of exposure before the next roll. In a flat market, the premium is the return. In a rising market, the fund keeps the premium and gives up everything past the strike. In months where the Russell 2000 runs hard enough to push the call in the money, the fund either lets the call settle in cash at expiration (RUT options are Euro-style, cash-settled on the index close) and pays out the difference or buys the call back before expiration at a loss to roll into a new position. Both outcomes compress NAV.

In a 2026 environment where the Russell 2000 has been breaking out, the fund mechanics are working against shareholders every single month.

Inside The ETF

The top 10 holdings of the fund are below:

Net Assets % Holding
103.30% Global X Russell 2000 ETF (RSSL)
0.26% USD Cash
0.00% Novartis AG (NVS)
0.00% Inhibrx Biosciences Inc. (INBX)
0.00% Pulse Biosciences, Inc. (PLSE)
0.00% CAD Cash
0.00% Cartesian Therapeutics, Inc. (RNAC)
0.00% OmniAb, Inc. (OABI) 12.5 Earnout Shares
0.00% OmniAb, Inc. (OABI) 15.00 Earnout Shares
0.00% Rain Oncology Inc. CVR
103.56% Total

13 Holdings

The fund is effectively a single-security wrapper with M&A residuals. That is the entire holding structure. The fund holds 103.30% in the Global X Russell 2000 ETF (RSSL), 0.26% in USD cash, and a handful of rounding-error residuals from historic Russell 2000 constituent M&A activity. The CVRs from Novartis, Inhibrx, and Rain Oncology are contingent value rights from past acquisitions. The OmniAb earnout shares are merger consideration artifacts. These are essentially stranded positions from holding the Russell 2000 through deal activity, and they round to zero on the balance sheet. The 103.30% allocation to RSSL exceeds 100% because of the short payable offset from the written call position on the other side of the book, which is standard for a covered call fund. For the investor, this means RYLD is not actively picking small caps. It is renting Russell 2000 exposure through RSSL and writing calls on top. The 0.60% expense ratio covers the management of the options overlay, and the options strategy itself produces premium income that is then distributed monthly.

Yields

Yield information pulled from the distributions section of the fund’s page states the following:

The 30-Day SEC yield is a standardized measure of the actual net investment income a fund is earning on its portfolio. It strips out option premiums, realized gains, and return of capital. For RYLD, that number is 1.56%. That is the real income generation rate on the underlying holdings.

The distribution rate is 11.62%. That is the annualized payout to shareholders. The gap between 1.56% and 11.62%, which is approximately 10 percentage points, is funded by option premiums and return of capital. The Global X fund page explicitly notes that the distribution is estimated to include a return of capital and directs readers to the 19a notice from March 23, 2026, for the breakdown.

Pure and persistent return of capital is not income (differentiated from occasional RoC on 19a notices to smooth out return reporting). It reduces the shareholder's cost basis. Over time, a fund that pays more in distributions than it earns in investment income is returning the shareholder's own money back to them while the NAV degrades. That is the pattern that RYLD has displayed since its inception.

Additionally, the 5Y dividend CAGR is -9.56%, and the 5Y share price CAGR is -9.16%. The 2Y numbers are similarly negative on both dividend (-5.97%) and price (-2.58%). The distribution is shrinking, and NAV is eroding at roughly the same pace, which is the signature of a covered call fund that is structurally paying more than it earns.

Small Cap Background For This Year

This is the section where the Sell rating is earned for RYLD. The Russell 2000 (RUT) is up approx. 11% YTD. The S&P 500 (SPX) is up by about 4%, with the NASDAQ (IXIC) up about 5.3%.

The rotation into small caps is happening on three catalysts, with the first one being valuation. The Russell 2000 trades at a forward P/E of approximately 18, against the S&P 500 at approximately 24. That gap is among the widest relative discounts in the last 25 years. Additionally, the Fed delivered 75 bps of rate cuts in late 2025, which disproportionately benefits smaller companies given that approximately 32-40% of Russell 2000 debt is floating rate, compared to less than 10% for the S&P 500.

Lastly, the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) restored 100% bonus depreciation and immediate domestic R&D expense, which is a bigger deal for capital-intensive small caps than it is for asset-light mega-caps. These are the tailwinds that Russell 2000 holders will capture. RYLD holders will capture them only to the strike price of the monthly call. For example, if IWM delivers 20% this year, RYLD should deliver roughly its distribution (approx. 12%) in total return, with NAV close to flat. That is 8%+ underperformance built mechanically into the fund structure.

JEPQ is evidence that covered call income ETFs can work when the structure is right. With JEPQ, the fund has delivered a 75.6% total return with DRIP over the past 4 years, which annualizes to a 15.11% CAGR, while paying a forward yield of 11.44%. That is almost the same headline yield as RYLD, but with more than 3x the total return. The structural difference is that JEPQ writes OTM calls via equity-linked notes (ELNs) against a quality-tilted Nasdaq 100 basket, which preserves meaningful upside participation. RYLD writes ATM calls against the entire position, which gives upside dollar-for-dollar past the strike.

IWMI is the most relevant upgrade trade for an RYLD holder who specifically wants Russell 2000 exposure with monthly income. IWMI holds Russell 2000 exposure directly through the underlying ETFs and constituent stocks and writes covered RUT index call options against that exposure. The options program is actively managed with a data-driven strike selection process. RUT options are classified as Section 1256 contracts, which receive 60/40 LTCG/STCG treatment regardless of holding period. On top of that, approx. 95% of IWMI distributions are classified as return of capital.

Tradability

RYLD trades approximately 1,279,370 shares per day at a NAV of $15.45, which translates to approximately $19.8 million in daily dollar volume. Fund AUM sits at $1.31 billion, with the primary class at $1.26 billion. The bid-ask spread is tight, which reflects the underlying holdings being liquid, and the fund itself has good daily flows, with daily volume being approximately 1.28 million. The tradability of this fund isn’t an issue, even if the strategy is risky for investors.

Upside Risks

The primary risk involves a sustained small-cap correction. If the Russell 2000 sells off on a growth scare, a rates shock, or geopolitical escalation, the monthly call premiums then become valuable downside cushion, and RYLD outperforms IWM on the way down. A 10-15% IWM drawdown would likely make RYLD relatively attractive on a peer comparison for one to two quarters.

Also, if implied volatility on the Russell 2000 spikes sharply, RYLD's monthly option premiums expand, which improves distribution coverage without needing ROC. An IV spike is the only scenario where this funds management style works in the shareholder’s favor without taking a NAV hit.

A less likely scenario would involve the Fed pivoting from rate cutting to rate hiking. This would hit small caps harder than large caps (given the previously discussed floating rate debt allocations to the respective indices), which again favors RYLD over an index-tracking ETF such as IWM.

Takeaway

RYLD is a Sell. The 12% distribution is the sales pitch, but 1.56% of that is actual investment income, and the rest is option premium mixed with return of capital. The strategy structurally gives the upside of the best small-cap setup in 15 years, and today's fund page confirms that the next 28 days of upside are already capped at 0.29%. If you desire RUT exposure, IWM is the cleaner trade at a fraction of the expense ratio. If you want monthly income from small caps specifically, IWMI has a better structure and has delivered a better return. If you want a covered call income ETF on a broad index, JEPQ is a better ETF, with a better strategy. For now, the rating is Sell, and the trade is to rotate out of RYLD.
